USER: Dostałem taką reklamację, pomóż mi ją przeanalizować:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
Reklamacja nr RK/2026/1184
Data: 22.03.2026

Nadawca: Agata Wiśniewska, Dział Zakupów
MediCall Sp. z o.o., ul. Szpitalna 12, Kraków

Dotyczy: Zamówienie ZAM-2026/0871 z dnia 04.03.2026
Produkt: System monitoringu pacjentów VitalTrack Pro (50 licencji)
Wartość: 287 500 PLN netto

Usterki:
1. Moduł SpO2 wyświetla wartości o 2-4% wyższe niż urządzenia referencyjne
2. Alarmy krytyczne (tachykardia) opóźnione o 8-12 sekund
3. Eksport danych do HL7 FHIR generuje nieprawidłowe zasoby Observation

Żądanie: Naprawa w ciągu 7 dni roboczych lub wymiana na działający system.
Podstawa: Gwarancja §7 ust. 3 umowy serwisowej US-2025/443.
---END DOCUMENT---

Co sądzisz o tej reklamacji?
ASSISTANT: To poważna reklamacja, szczególnie w kontekście medycznym. Błąd SpO2 o 2-4% i opóźnienie alarmów o 8-12 sekund mogą mieć konsekwencje kliniczne. To nie jest zwykły bug w oprogramowaniu — to potencjalne zagrożenie dla pacjentów. Usterka z HL7 FHIR jest mniej krytyczna, ale blokuje integrację.
USER: Mamy 7 dni na naprawę. Czy to realistyczne?
ASSISTANT: Usterka SpO2 prawdopodobnie wymaga kalibracji firmware — 2-3 dni. Opóźnienie alarmów to może być problem z kolejkowaniem eventów — trudniejsze, ale 3-4 dni. HL7 FHIR to kwestia mapowania — 1-2 dni. Łącznie na styk, ale wykonalne przy dedykowanym zespole.
USER: Problem w tym, że nasz zespół firmware jest zajęty projektem dla wojska do końca kwietnia. Mamy tylko frontend devów.
ASSISTANT: W takim razie musicie negocjować wydłużenie terminu lub zatrudnić zewnętrznego specjalistę firmware. Frontend devowie nie naprawią kalibracji SpO2.
USER: Zgadza się. Chcę odpowiedzieć że potrzebujemy 21 dni. Czy klient się zgodzi?
ASSISTANT: Biorąc pod uwagę kontekst medyczny, klient raczej nie zaakceptuje 21 dni bez dodatkowych gwarancji. Proponuję zaoferować tymczasowe obejście — wyłączenie wadliwego modułu SpO2 i ręczny monitoring, plus priorytetową naprawę alarmów w 7 dni, a resztę w 21 dni.
USER: Dobry pomysł z fazowaniem. Alarmy najpierw, reszta potem.
output:
TEMAT: Reklamacja systemu monitoringu pacjentów VitalTrack Pro — usterki krytyczne
DOKUMENT_ŹRÓDŁOWY: Reklamacja nr RK/2026/1184 od MediCall Sp. z o.o. z dnia 22.03.2026
KLUCZOWE_FAKTY_Z_DOKUMENTU:
- Zamówienie: ZAM-2026/0871 z 04.03.2026
- Produkt: VitalTrack Pro, 50 licencji, 287 500 PLN netto
- Usterka 1: SpO2 zawyżone o 2-4% vs urządzenia referencyjne
- Usterka 2: Alarmy tachykardii opóźnione 8-12 sekund
- Usterka 3: Eksport HL7 FHIR — nieprawidłowe zasoby Observation
- Żądanie: naprawa 7 dni roboczych lub wymiana
- Podstawa: gwarancja §7 ust. 3 umowy US-2025/443
USTALENIA_Z_ROZMOWY:
- Usterki medycznie krytyczne — zagrożenie dla pacjentów
- Zespół firmware niedostępny (projekt wojskowy do końca kwietnia)
- Dostępni tylko frontend devowie — nie mogą naprawić firmware
- 7 dni nierealistyczne przy obecnych zasobach
- Propozycja fazowania: alarmy w 7 dni, reszta w 21 dni
- Tymczasowe obejście: wyłączenie SpO2 + ręczny monitoring
FEEDBACK_UŻYTKOWNIKA:
- "Problem w tym, że nasz zespół firmware jest zajęty" (korekta założeń AI o zasobach)
- "Dobry pomysł z fazowaniem" (akceptacja propozycji AI)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Przygotować odpowiedź do MediCall z planem fazowym
- Naprawić alarmy krytyczne w 7 dni
- Znaleźć zewnętrznego specjalistę firmware lub zwolnić zasoby
DECYZJE: Fazowanie naprawy — alarmy priorytet (7 dni), SpO2 i HL7 FHIR (21 dni)

input:
USER: Przeczytaj ten raport z pentestu i powiedz co jest najgroźniejsze:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
RAPORT Z TESTÓW PENETRACYJNYCH
Zleceniodawca: FinSecure S.A.
Cel: Aplikacja webowa — Portal Klienta (portal.finsecure.pl)
Data testów: 10-14.03.2026
Metodologia: OWASP Testing Guide v4.2

Wyniki:

KRYTYCZNE (CVSS 9.0+):
[K1] SQL Injection w /api/v2/transactions?account_id=
  - Payload: ' UNION SELECT username,password_hash,null FROM users--
  - Skutek: Pełen dostęp do bazy danych, exfiltracja haseł
  - CVSS: 9.8

[K2] Broken Authentication — JWT bez weryfikacji podpisu
  - Header: {"alg":"none"}
  - Skutek: Przejęcie dowolnej sesji użytkownika
  - CVSS: 9.1

WYSOKIE (CVSS 7.0-8.9):
[W1] IDOR na /api/v2/accounts/{id}/balance
  - Zmiana ID w URL daje dostęp do salda innych klientów
  - CVSS: 7.5

[W2] XSS Stored w module czatu
  - Payload w polu wiadomości wykonywany u agenta obsługi
  - CVSS: 7.2

ŚREDNIE (CVSS 4.0-6.9):
[S1] Brak rate-limitingu na /api/v2/auth/login
  - Brute-force możliwy
  - CVSS: 5.3
---END DOCUMENT---

Co naprawiać najpierw?
ASSISTANT: Bezwzględnie K1 (SQL Injection) i K2 (JWT none algorithm) — oba dają pełen dostęp do systemu. K1 to klasyczny SQLi z exfiltracją haseł, K2 pozwala przejąć dowolną sesję. To portal finansowy, więc te podatności mogą oznaczać naruszenie danych osobowych i sankcje KNF.
USER: K2 naprawiliśmy wczoraj, dodaliśmy wymuszenie RS256.
ASSISTANT: Dobrze. Teraz K1 — musicie przejść na parametryzowane zapytania w endpoincie transactions. Potem W1 (IDOR) bo też daje dostęp do danych finansowych.
USER: A W2 ze stored XSS w czacie? Agent obsługi ma dostęp do panelu admin.
ASSISTANT: To podnosi ryzyko W2 — XSS wykonywany w kontekście agenta z uprawnieniami admina to de facto eskalacja uprawnień. Przesuńbym go wyżej w priorytecie, równolegle z IDOR.
USER: Zgadzam się. Kolejność: K1 → W1+W2 równolegle → S1.
ASSISTANT: Potwierdzone. Przy S1 dodajcie rate-limiting plus mechanizm lockout po N nieudanych próbach.
USER: Lockout mamy, ale ustawiony na 50 prób. Za dużo?
ASSISTANT: Zdecydowanie. Standard to 5-10 prób, potem lockout na 15-30 minut. 50 prób to za mało ochrony przed brute-force.
USER: OK, zmieniamy na 5 prób i 30 minut lockouta.
output:
TEMAT: Analiza raportu z pentestów portalu klienta FinSecure — plan naprawy
DOKUMENT_ŹRÓDŁOWY: Raport z testów penetracyjnych portal.finsecure.pl, 10-14.03.2026
KLUCZOWE_FAKTY_Z_DOKUMENTU:
- [K1] SQL Injection w /api/v2/transactions — CVSS 9.8, exfiltracja haseł
- [K2] JWT bez weryfikacji podpisu (alg:none) — CVSS 9.1, przejęcie sesji
- [W1] IDOR /api/v2/accounts/{id}/balance — CVSS 7.5, dostęp do sald
- [W2] Stored XSS w czacie — CVSS 7.2, wykonanie u agenta obsługi
- [S1] Brak rate-limitingu na login — CVSS 5.3, brute-force
USTALENIA_Z_ROZMOWY:
- K2 już naprawione (wymuszenie RS256)
- W2 groźniejszy niż CVSS sugeruje — agent ma uprawnienia admina (eskalacja)
- Istniejący lockout: 50 prób — za dużo, zmiana na 5 prób + 30 min
FEEDBACK_UŻYTKOWNIKA:
- "K2 naprawiliśmy wczoraj" (korekta stanu — AI nie wiedział)
- "Agent obsługi ma dostęp do panelu admin" (korekta priorytetu W2)
- "Zgadzam się. Kolejność: K1 → W1+W2 → S1" (decyzja)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Naprawić K1: parametryzowane zapytania w /api/v2/transactions
- Naprawić W1 (IDOR) i W2 (XSS) równolegle
- Zmienić lockout z 50 na 5 prób + 30 min
- Dodać rate-limiting na /api/v2/auth/login
DECYZJE: Kolejność: K1 → W1+W2 równolegle → S1. Lockout: 5 prób / 30 min

input:
USER: Review this SLA document from our cloud vendor and tell me if we're protected:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
SERVICE LEVEL AGREEMENT
Provider: NimbusCloud Inc.
Customer: Orion Digital Ltd.
Effective: January 1, 2026

Section 3 — Uptime Commitment:
3.1 Provider guarantees 99.9% monthly uptime for Compute services.
3.2 Uptime is measured as: ((Total Minutes - Excluded Downtime - Unplanned Downtime) / (Total Minutes - Excluded Downtime)) × 100
3.3 Excluded Downtime includes: scheduled maintenance (up to 8 hours/month with 48h notice), force majeure, customer-caused outages.

Section 4 — Service Credits:
4.1 If uptime falls below 99.9%: 10% credit on monthly Compute bill
4.2 If uptime falls below 99.0%: 25% credit
4.3 If uptime falls below 95.0%: 50% credit
4.4 Maximum aggregate credits per month: 50% of monthly Compute charges
4.5 Credits must be requested within 30 days of the incident
4.6 Credits are applied to future invoices, not refunded

Section 5 — Exclusions:
5.1 SLA does not apply to: beta features, free tier, services in preview
5.2 SLA void if customer exceeds published API rate limits
5.3 Provider not liable for data loss; customer responsible for backups
---END DOCUMENT---

Are we okay with this?
ASSISTANT: There are several concerns. The uptime calculation in 3.2 excludes scheduled maintenance (up to 8h/month) — that's 96 hours/year of planned downtime not counted against SLA. Real uptime could be significantly lower than 99.9%. The credit structure caps at 50% — even a full outage only gets you half off. And 5.3 is a major red flag: they disclaim all liability for data loss.
USER: The 8 hours maintenance window — is that normal?
ASSISTANT: It's on the high side. AWS and Azure typically have zero scheduled maintenance windows for compute (they do live migration). 8 hours/month is more typical of smaller providers. That said, 48h notice is reasonable.
USER: What about 5.2? We hit rate limits sometimes during batch jobs.
ASSISTANT: That's dangerous. Section 5.2 voids the entire SLA if you exceed rate limits — not just for that service, but seemingly all services. One batch job hitting rate limits could void your uptime guarantee for everything. You need to negotiate this to only apply to the specific service that exceeded limits.
USER: Good catch. And 5.3 about data loss — we don't have our own backup strategy yet.
ASSISTANT: That's critical. Section 5.3 means if NimbusCloud loses your data, they owe you nothing. You need to implement your own backup strategy immediately, regardless of SLA negotiations. Cross-region replication at minimum.
USER: Right. Let me summarize what we need to push back on.
output:
TOPIC: Review of NimbusCloud SLA — risk assessment for Orion Digital
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Service Level Agreement, NimbusCloud Inc. → Orion Digital Ltd., effective Jan 1, 2026
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Uptime: 99.9% monthly for Compute
- Uptime formula excludes scheduled maintenance (up to 8h/month, 48h notice) and force majeure
- Credits: 10% (<99.9%), 25% (<99.0%), 50% (<95.0%), capped at 50% of monthly Compute
- Credits must be requested within 30 days, applied to future invoices only
- SLA void if customer exceeds API rate limits (Section 5.2)
- Provider not liable for data loss; customer responsible for backups (Section 5.3)
- SLA excludes beta, free tier, preview services
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- 8h/month maintenance exclusion is high vs industry standard (AWS/Azure: zero)
- Real uptime significantly lower than 99.9% when maintenance is factored in
- Section 5.2 voids ENTIRE SLA on rate limit breach — not scoped to affected service
- Customer currently hits rate limits during batch jobs — immediate risk
- Section 5.3 is critical: no data loss liability and customer has NO backup strategy
- Credit cap at 50% is low; no refund option, only future invoice credits
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "We hit rate limits sometimes during batch jobs" (revealed operational risk for 5.2)
- "we don't have our own backup strategy yet" (revealed critical gap for 5.3)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Negotiate Section 5.2: scope SLA void to affected service only, not all services
- Negotiate Section 3.3: reduce maintenance window from 8h to 2-4h/month
- Negotiate Section 5.3: add data durability commitment or liability clause
- Implement backup strategy immediately (cross-region replication minimum)
- Review rate limit usage during batch jobs to avoid SLA voidance
DECISIONS: Push back on Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 3.3 maintenance window

input:
USER: I need to review this architecture proposal before our Monday meeting. Can you help?

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
ARCHITECTURE PROPOSAL: Event-Driven Order Processing System
Author: Sarah Chen, Staff Engineer
Date: March 20, 2026
Status: RFC

1. Current State:
- Monolithic Django app handling 2,000 orders/day
- Synchronous processing: order → payment → inventory → shipping
- Average latency: 4.2 seconds per order
- Single PostgreSQL 13 database (16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 85% utilization)

2. Proposed Architecture:
- Event-driven microservices using Apache Kafka
- Services: OrderService, PaymentService, InventoryService, ShippingService
- Each service owns its data (PostgreSQL per service)
- Event sourcing for order state with CQRS read models
- Eventual consistency between services

3. Technology Stack:
- Runtime: Go 1.22 for all services
- Messaging: Apache Kafka (3 brokers, 3 ZK nodes)
- Database: PostgreSQL 16 per service
- Cache: Redis Cluster (6 nodes)
- Orchestration: Kubernetes (EKS)
- Observability: OpenTelemetry → Jaeger + Prometheus + Grafana

4. Expected Outcomes:
- Latency: <500ms per order (p99)
- Throughput: 50,000 orders/day capacity
- Availability: 99.99%
---END DOCUMENT---

Is this solid?
ASSISTANT: The proposal is technically sound but raises some concerns. Going from a monolith processing 2,000 orders/day to a full Kafka + event sourcing + CQRS architecture designed for 50,000/day is a massive jump — 25x capacity. That's significant over-engineering unless there's a clear growth trajectory.
USER: We're expecting 10x growth in the next 18 months due to a partnership deal.
ASSISTANT: 10x growth means ~20,000 orders/day. The proposed 50,000 capacity gives you 2.5x headroom, which is reasonable. But you could achieve 20k orders/day with a simpler architecture — perhaps just async processing with a task queue (Celery/SQS) and read replicas, without full event sourcing and CQRS.
USER: Sarah insists on event sourcing. She says we need the audit trail.
ASSISTANT: Event sourcing for audit trails is valid but expensive in complexity. An alternative is plain event logging — publish events to Kafka for the audit trail, but keep the services using simple CRUD with their own databases. You get the audit trail without the complexity of rebuilding state from events.
USER: That's a good middle ground. What about the Go rewrite? Our team is 8 Python developers.
ASSISTANT: Rewriting from Python to Go while simultaneously decomposing the monolith into microservices is extremely risky. You're changing architecture, language, deployment model, and database topology all at once. I'd strongly recommend keeping Python (FastAPI) and focusing on the architecture change.
USER: Agreed. Python stays. What else should I flag?
ASSISTANT: Three things: 1) PostgreSQL per service means distributed transactions — how do you handle order+payment atomicity? Saga pattern? 2) Redis Cluster with 6 nodes for 20k orders/day is overkill. 3) ZooKeeper-based Kafka is legacy — use KRaft mode.
USER: Good points. I'll bring these up Monday.
output:
TOPIC: Review of event-driven order processing architecture proposal (RFC by Sarah Chen)
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Architecture Proposal — Event-Driven Order Processing System, by Sarah Chen, March 20, 2026
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Current: Django monolith, 2,000 orders/day, 4.2s latency, single PostgreSQL 13 at 85% util
- Proposed: Kafka event-driven microservices (Order, Payment, Inventory, Shipping)
- Event sourcing + CQRS, database-per-service, eventual consistency
- Stack: Go 1.22, Kafka (3 brokers + 3 ZK), PostgreSQL 16 per service, Redis Cluster (6 nodes), EKS
- Targets: <500ms p99, 50,000 orders/day, 99.99% availability
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- 50k/day capacity for expected 20k/day (10x growth) — 2.5x headroom is OK
- Full event sourcing unnecessary — event logging to Kafka provides audit trail with less complexity
- Go rewrite + microservices decomposition simultaneously is too risky for Python team (8 devs)
- PostgreSQL per service needs distributed transaction strategy (Saga pattern)
- Redis Cluster 6 nodes overkill for 20k orders/day
- ZooKeeper-based Kafka is legacy; use KRaft mode
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "We're expecting 10x growth" (context AI didn't have — justified scale)
- "Sarah insists on event sourcing" for audit trail (AI proposed middle ground)
- "Agreed. Python stays" (accepted AI recommendation against Go rewrite)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Flag at Monday meeting: keep Python (FastAPI), drop Go rewrite
- Propose event logging instead of full event sourcing
- Discuss Saga pattern for cross-service transaction atomicity
- Right-size Redis (smaller cluster) and use Kafka KRaft mode
- Clarify growth projections with partnership team
DECISIONS: Keep Python, simplify event sourcing to event logging, flag distributed transaction strategy

USER: Can you look at this incident postmortem and tell me if the action items are sufficient?

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
INCIDENT POSTMORTEM — INC-2026-0342
Severity: SEV-1
Duration: 3 hours 47 minutes (March 18, 2026, 14:23–18:10 UTC)
Impact: Complete API outage for all customers (~4,200 affected accounts)

Timeline:
14:23 — Monitoring alert: API error rate >50%
14:31 — On-call engineer acknowledges, begins investigation
14:45 — Root cause identified: OOM on all 6 API pods after deployment v2.14.3
14:52 — Rollback initiated (v2.14.2)
15:15 — Rollback fails: database migration in v2.14.3 is not backward-compatible
15:15-17:30 — Team attempts forward-fix, writing SQL to manually revert migration
17:30 — Forward-fix deployed, migration reverted
17:45 — API pods restarted with v2.14.2
18:10 — Full recovery confirmed

Root Cause:
v2.14.3 included a database migration that added a JSON column with a NOT NULL constraint and DEFAULT value. PostgreSQL materialized the default across 42M rows during ALTER TABLE, consuming all available memory on API pods (shared connection pool exhaustion).

Action Items:
1. Add memory limits to API pods (P1, DevOps, due: March 25)
2. Require staging deployment for all migrations (P1, Engineering, due: April 1)
3. Update runbook for database rollback procedures (P2, SRE, due: April 15)
---END DOCUMENT---

Are these action items enough?
ASSISTANT: No, they're insufficient. The action items address symptoms but miss the core problem. The real issue was a migration that materialized defaults across 42M rows — adding memory limits won't prevent that, it'll just change the failure mode from OOM to connection timeouts. Key missing items: 1) Migration review process to catch expensive DDL operations, 2) Non-locking migration strategy (e.g., add column as nullable, backfill in batches, then add constraint), 3) Rollback testing requirement before any migration deploys.
USER: Good points. What about the 8-minute gap between alert and acknowledgment?
ASSISTANT: 14:23 alert to 14:31 ack is 8 minutes. For a SEV-1, that's borderline. Industry standard is under 5 minutes. Could be improved with better escalation policy or PagerDuty auto-escalation at 5 minutes.
USER: And the rollback took from 15:15 to 17:45 — over 2 hours. That's the real problem.
ASSISTANT: Exactly. The rollback failed because the migration was irreversible. The 2h15m gap was spent writing ad-hoc SQL under pressure. Action item 3 (runbook update) doesn't prevent this — you need a requirement that every migration ships with a tested rollback script. No rollback script = no deploy.
USER: What about the 4,200 affected customers? There's no communication action item.
ASSISTANT: Great catch. There's nothing about customer communication, status page updates during the incident, or a post-incident customer notification. For a nearly 4-hour complete outage, that's a significant gap.
USER: Let me rewrite the action items based on this discussion.
output:
TOPIC: Review of SEV-1 incident postmortem INC-2026-0342 — API outage action items
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Incident Postmortem INC-2026-0342, March 18, 2026
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- SEV-1, 3h47m total outage (14:23–18:10 UTC), 4,200 accounts affected
- Root cause: v2.14.3 migration materialized DEFAULT across 42M rows → OOM on API pods
- Rollback failed: migration not backward-compatible, 2h15m spent on manual SQL revert
- Alert-to-ack: 8 minutes (14:23→14:31)
- Original action items: memory limits (P1), staging migrations (P1), runbook update (P2)
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Action items address symptoms, not root cause
- Memory limits change failure mode (OOM → timeout), don't prevent expensive migrations
- Missing: migration review process for expensive DDL operations
- Missing: non-locking migration strategy (nullable → backfill → constraint)
- Missing: mandatory tested rollback script per migration
- 8-minute ack time borderline for SEV-1 (standard: <5 min)
- 2h15m rollback gap is the critical failure — ad-hoc SQL under pressure
- No customer communication action item for 4h outage affecting 4,200 accounts
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "the rollback took over 2 hours — That's the real problem" (shifted focus to core issue)
- "There's no communication action item" (AI missed customer-facing gap)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Rewrite postmortem action items to include:
  - Migration review process (catch expensive DDL)
  - Non-locking migration pattern requirement
  - Mandatory rollback scripts tested before deploy
  - PagerDuty auto-escalation at 5 min for SEV-1
  - Customer communication protocol for outages
  - Status page update process during incidents
DECISIONS: User rewriting action items based on expanded analysis

input:
USER: I got this contract amendment from our legal team. I need to understand the IP implications before I sign:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
AMENDMENT NO. 3 TO MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT
Between: Vertex Analytics Inc. ("Client") and DataForge LLC ("Contractor")
Original Agreement Date: June 15, 2024
Amendment Date: March 22, 2026

Section 8.2 (Intellectual Property) is hereby replaced in its entirety:

8.2.1 Work Product. All work product, inventions, code, algorithms, models, and documentation created by Contractor in the performance of Services ("Work Product") shall be the exclusive property of Client. Contractor hereby assigns all rights, title, and interest in Work Product to Client.

8.2.2 Pre-Existing IP. Contractor retains ownership of tools, libraries, frameworks, and methodologies that existed prior to the Effective Date or are developed independently outside the scope of Services ("Pre-Existing IP"). Contractor grants Client a perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free license to use Pre-Existing IP solely as incorporated in Work Product.

8.2.3 AI-Generated Content. Any content generated using artificial intelligence tools in the performance of Services shall be treated as Work Product under 8.2.1, regardless of the degree of human contribution.

8.2.4 Non-Compete. During the term and for 24 months thereafter, Contractor shall not develop substantially similar solutions for direct competitors of Client, as listed in Exhibit D.
---END DOCUMENT---

Is this standard?
ASSISTANT: This is mostly standard for contractor agreements, but with two notable clauses. 8.2.3 (AI-Generated Content) is increasingly common but worth scrutiny — it means anything you generate with AI tools (Copilot, ChatGPT, etc.) during the project belongs to the client entirely. And 8.2.4 is a 24-month non-compete, which is quite long for a contractor.
USER: I use my own custom ML pipeline toolkit that I've built over 5 years. Does 8.2.2 protect me?
ASSISTANT: Partially. 8.2.2 protects pre-existing IP, but you need to explicitly list your ML toolkit in an exhibit or schedule. If it's not documented as pre-existing, it could be claimed as Work Product under 8.2.1. Also, 8.2.2 grants the client a perpetual, irrevocable license to your toolkit as incorporated — meaning they can use it forever in the deliverables.
USER: What if I improve my toolkit while working on their project?
ASSISTANT: That's the grey area. Improvements made "in the performance of Services" would be Work Product under 8.2.1. Improvements made independently would be your Pre-Existing IP. The line is hard to draw. You should negotiate a clause explicitly stating that improvements to Pre-Existing IP remain yours.
USER: And the AI clause 8.2.3 — I use Copilot for everything. Does that mean ALL my code becomes their property?
ASSISTANT: Only code created "in the performance of Services." But yes, if you use Copilot while working on their project, the output is their Work Product under both 8.2.1 and 8.2.3. The concern is if you use Copilot on your own toolkit while also working on their project — the lines blur.
USER: This is getting complicated. What should I push back on?
output:
TOPIC: IP implications of contract amendment — DataForge/Vertex Analytics MSA Amendment 3
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Amendment No. 3 to MSA between Vertex Analytics Inc. and DataForge LLC, dated March 22, 2026
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Section 8.2.1: All Work Product assigned exclusively to Client
- Section 8.2.2: Pre-Existing IP retained by Contractor; Client gets perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license for Pre-Existing IP as incorporated
- Section 8.2.3: AI-generated content treated as Work Product regardless of human contribution
- Section 8.2.4: 24-month non-compete for substantially similar solutions, competitors in Exhibit D
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Contractor has custom ML pipeline toolkit (5 years of development) — must be explicitly listed as Pre-Existing IP
- Improvements to Pre-Existing IP during engagement fall in grey area (could be claimed as Work Product)
- AI clause (8.2.3) + heavy Copilot usage = all AI-assisted code during Services becomes Client property
- Risk: using Copilot on own toolkit while also performing Services blurs ownership
- 24-month non-compete is long for contractor agreements
- Pre-Existing IP license is perpetual and irrevocable — Client keeps usage rights forever
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "I use my own custom ML pipeline toolkit" (critical context AI didn't have)
- "What if I improve my toolkit while working on their project?" (identified key grey area)
- "I use Copilot for everything" (amplifies risk of 8.2.3)
ACTION_ITEMS:
- List ML toolkit explicitly in Pre-Existing IP exhibit before signing
- Negotiate: improvements to Pre-Existing IP remain Contractor's property
- Negotiate: narrow 8.2.3 to AI outputs created specifically for Client deliverables
- Negotiate: reduce non-compete from 24 to 12 months
- Consider: separate development environments for Client work vs own toolkit
DECISIONS: Push back on 8.2.3 scope, 8.2.4 duration, and add Pre-Existing IP improvement clause

USER: Ich habe dieses Angebot für unser neues Büro bekommen. Kannst du es prüfen?

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
MIETANGEBOT
Vermieter: Gewerbepark Süd GmbH, Industriestraße 88, 80939 München
Mieter: TechFlow Solutions GmbH

Objekt: Bürofläche 3. OG, Bauteil C
Fläche: 450 m² (340 m² Büro + 110 m² Gemeinschaftsflächen)
Stellplätze: 12 TG-Stellplätze

Mietkonditionen:
- Nettokaltmiete: 19,50 EUR/m²/Monat (= 8.775 EUR/Monat)
- Nebenkosten-Vorauszahlung: 4,80 EUR/m²/Monat (= 2.160 EUR/Monat)
- TG-Stellplätze: 95 EUR/Stück/Monat (= 1.140 EUR/Monat)
- Gesamtmiete brutto (inkl. MwSt.): ca. 14.369 EUR/Monat

Vertragslaufzeit: 5 Jahre, Verlängerung um jeweils 2 Jahre bei Nichtkündigung
Kündigungsfrist: 9 Monate zum Vertragsende
Mietfreie Zeit: 3 Monate
Ausbau: Vermieter übernimmt Standardausbau bis 150 EUR/m² (= 67.500 EUR)
Kaution: 3 Monatsmieten netto (= 36.225 EUR)
Indexierung: 100% des VPI, Anpassung bei Änderung >5%
---END DOCUMENT---

Was denkst du?
ASSISTANT: Das Angebot liegt im Rahmen für München-Nord (Industriestraße). 19,50 EUR/m² ist marktüblich für Gewerbeparks außerhalb des Zentrums. Die mietfreie Zeit von 3 Monaten ist bei 5 Jahren Laufzeit akzeptabel, aber verhandelbar auf 4-6 Monate. Der Ausbau-Zuschuss von 150 EUR/m² ist knapp — für einen modernen Büroausbau rechnet man eher mit 200-300 EUR/m².
USER: Die 110 m² Gemeinschaftsflächen — werden die voll berechnet?
ASSISTANT: Ja, die 450 m² beinhalten 110 m² Gemeinschaftsflächen, die zum gleichen Preis berechnet werden. Das ist üblich aber ungünstig. Sie könnten verhandeln, dass Gemeinschaftsflächen nur anteilig (z.B. 50%) berechnet werden, was die effektive Miete senkt.
USER: Guter Punkt. Und die Indexierung — 100% VPI ist doch viel?
ASSISTANT: 100% VPI-Indexierung bedeutet, dass jede Inflationssteigerung voll auf die Miete durchschlägt. Üblich sind 60-80%. Bei der aktuellen Inflation könnte das in 5 Jahren erheblich werden. Verhandeln Sie auf 70% herunter.
USER: Wir sind 35 Mitarbeiter. Reichen 340 m² Bürofläche?
ASSISTANT: 340 m² für 35 Mitarbeiter ergibt ca. 9,7 m² pro Person. Das ist knapp — der Standard liegt bei 10-12 m² für moderne Büros. Wenn Sie wachsen wollen, wird es eng.
USER: Wir planen auf 50 MA in 2 Jahren zu wachsen.
ASSISTANT: Dann sind 340 m² definitiv zu klein — 50 MA × 10 m² = 500 m² Bürofläche nötig. Sie müssten entweder eine Expansionsoption verhandeln oder eine größere Fläche suchen.
USER: OK, ich werde nach Expansionsoptionen fragen.
output:
THEMA: Prüfung Mietangebot Gewerbepark Süd München für TechFlow Solutions
QUELLDOKUMENT: Mietangebot von Gewerbepark Süd GmbH, Industriestraße 88, München
KERNFAKTEN_AUS_DOKUMENT:
- Fläche: 450 m² (340 m² Büro + 110 m² Gemeinschaft), 3. OG Bauteil C
- Nettokaltmiete: 19,50 EUR/m²/Monat (8.775 EUR/Monat)
- NK-Vorauszahlung: 4,80 EUR/m², Stellplätze: 12× 95 EUR
- Gesamtmiete brutto: ca. 14.369 EUR/Monat
- Laufzeit: 5 Jahre + 2J-Verlängerung, Kündigung 9 Monate
- Mietfrei: 3 Monate, Ausbau: 150 EUR/m² (67.500 EUR), Kaution: 36.225 EUR
- Indexierung: 100% VPI bei >5% Änderung
ERKENNTNISSE_AUS_GESPRÄCH:
- 19,50 EUR/m² marktüblich für München-Nord (Gewerbepark)
- 110 m² Gemeinschaftsflächen voll berechnet — verhandelbar auf 50%
- 100% VPI-Indexierung zu hoch — Standard 60-80%, verhandeln auf 70%
- Ausbau-Zuschuss 150 EUR/m² knapp (Standard: 200-300 EUR/m²)
- 340 m² für 35 MA = 9,7 m²/Person — knapp
- Wachstumsplan: 50 MA in 2 Jahren → 500 m² nötig → 340 m² zu klein
- Mietfreie Zeit verhandelbar auf 4-6 Monate
NUTZER_FEEDBACK:
- "Reichen 340 m²?" → AI bestätigte: zu knapp
- "Wir planen auf 50 MA" (kritischer Kontext — Fläche unzureichend)
MASSNAHMEN:
- Expansionsoption verhandeln (Nachbarfläche, Vorkaufsrecht)
- Gemeinschaftsflächen-Anteil auf 50% verhandeln
- VPI-Indexierung auf 70% herunterverhandeln
- Mietfreie Zeit auf 4-6 Monate verhandeln
- Ausbau-Zuschuss auf 200+ EUR/m² verhandeln
ENTSCHEIDUNGEN: Verhandlung über Expansionsoption priorisiert wegen Wachstumsplanung auf 50 MA

USER: J'ai reçu ce rapport d'audit RGPD. Dis-moi ce qui est urgent:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
RAPPORT D'AUDIT RGPD
Entité auditée: MédiaPlus SAS
Auditeur: Cabinet Dupont & Associés
Date de l'audit: 4-8 mars 2026
Périmètre: Plateforme publicitaire programmatique + CRM

NON-CONFORMITÉS MAJEURES:

[NC-01] Absence de base légale pour le profilage publicitaire
- Constat: Le profilage des utilisateurs (centres d'intérêt, habitudes de navigation) est effectué sans consentement explicite ni intérêt légitime documenté
- Impact: ~2,3 millions de profils utilisateurs concernés
- Risque: Amende jusqu'à 4% du CA mondial (Art. 83§5 RGPD)
- Recommandation: Mettre en place une CMP conforme et requalifier la base légale

[NC-02] Transferts hors UE non encadrés
- Constat: Données utilisateurs transférées vers serveurs AWS us-east-1 sans clauses contractuelles types (CCT) ni évaluation d'impact du transfert
- Impact: Toutes les données de la plateforme
- Risque: Invalidation des traitements + amende
- Recommandation: Migrer vers eu-west-1 ou mettre en place CCT + TIA

[NC-03] Durée de conservation excessive
- Constat: Données de navigation conservées 7 ans (politique interne: 2 ans, aucune purge automatisée)
- Impact: ~18 To de données à purger
- Recommandation: Implémenter purge automatisée et supprimer l'historique >2 ans

NON-CONFORMITÉS MINEURES:
[NC-04] Registre des traitements incomplet
[NC-05] Délai de réponse aux demandes d'accès >30 jours (moyenne: 47 jours)
---END DOCUMENT---

Qu'est-ce qui est le plus urgent?
ASSISTANT: NC-01 est le plus urgent et le plus risqué. Le profilage de 2,3 millions d'utilisateurs sans base légale est une violation flagrante — c'est exactement le type de cas que la CNIL sanctionne lourdement (cf. amendes Criteo, Google). L'amende peut atteindre 4% du CA mondial.
USER: On utilise le consentement implicite via la bannière cookies. C'est pas suffisant?
ASSISTANT: Non. Depuis les lignes directrices de la CNIL de 2020 et le cadre ePrivacy, le consentement pour le profilage publicitaire doit être explicite, libre, éclairé et spécifique. Une bannière avec "En continuant, vous acceptez" n'est pas un consentement valide. Il faut un opt-in actif.
USER: Et NC-02 — on peut juste migrer vers eu-west-1?
ASSISTANT: C'est la solution la plus simple et la plus propre. La migration vers eu-west-1 (Irlande) élimine complètement le problème de transfert hors UE. Les CCT + TIA sont une alternative mais plus complexes à maintenir, surtout post-Schrems II.
USER: Le problème c'est que notre pipeline de machine learning est sur us-east-1 avec des GPU spécifiques.
ASSISTANT: Dans ce cas, deux options: 1) Anonymiser les données avant le transfert vers us-east-1 pour le ML (données anonymisées ne sont plus des données personnelles au sens du RGPD), ou 2) Utiliser eu-west-1 pour le stockage et traitement des données personnelles, avec un pipeline anonymisé qui alimente le ML aux US.
USER: L'option 2 est faisable. Et pour NC-03, 18 To à purger — c'est risqué techniquement?
ASSISTANT: La purge de 18 To nécessite de la planification mais n'est pas risquée si vous faites une sauvegarde avant, purgez par lots (pas tout d'un coup), et validez avec le juridique quelles données ont des obligations de conservation légales (facturation, contrats). Faites-le pendant une fenêtre de maintenance.
USER: OK, plan d'action: NC-01 d'abord, puis NC-02, puis NC-03.
output:
SUJET: Audit RGPD MédiaPlus — non-conformités majeures et plan de remédiation
DOCUMENT_SOURCE: Rapport d'audit RGPD par Cabinet Dupont & Associés, 4-8 mars 2026
FAITS_CLÉS_DU_DOCUMENT:
- [NC-01] Profilage de 2,3M utilisateurs sans base légale — risque: 4% CA mondial
- [NC-02] Transferts hors UE (AWS us-east-1) sans CCT ni TIA
- [NC-03] Conservation 7 ans au lieu de 2 ans — 18 To à purger
- [NC-04] Registre des traitements incomplet (mineur)
- [NC-05] Délai réponse accès 47 jours vs 30 jours requis (mineur)
CONSTATS_DE_LA_DISCUSSION:
- Bannière cookies avec consentement implicite insuffisante — opt-in actif requis (CNIL 2020)
- Migration eu-west-1 est la solution la plus propre pour NC-02
- Pipeline ML sur us-east-1 avec GPU spécifiques — contrainte technique
- Solution retenue: stockage/traitement données personnelles en eu-west-1, pipeline anonymisé vers US
- Purge 18 To faisable par lots avec sauvegarde préalable et validation juridique
FEEDBACK_UTILISATEUR:
- "On utilise le consentement implicite" (révèle la non-conformité active)
- "notre pipeline ML est sur us-east-1 avec des GPU spécifiques" (contrainte technique pour NC-02)
- "L'option 2 est faisable" (validation de l'approche hybride)
ACTIONS:
- NC-01: Implémenter CMP conforme avec opt-in actif, requalifier base légale
- NC-02: Migrer données personnelles vers eu-west-1, anonymiser avant transfert ML
- NC-03: Purge par lots des données >2 ans avec sauvegarde et validation juridique
DÉCISIONS: Priorité NC-01 → NC-02 → NC-03. Architecture hybride EU/US avec anonymisation

input:
USER: Necesito que revises esta propuesta de migración de base de datos:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
PROPUESTA DE MIGRACIÓN
Proyecto: Migración Oracle 12c → PostgreSQL 16
Cliente: BancoSur S.A.
Consultor: DataMigrate Solutions

1. ALCANCE:
- 847 tablas, 2.3 TB de datos
- 1,200 procedimientos almacenados PL/SQL → PL/pgSQL
- 340 jobs Oracle → pg_cron
- 89 materialized views
- Integraciones: 12 servicios REST, 4 feeds batch (ETL), 2 conexiones SWIFT

2. FASES:
Fase 1 (Sem 1-4): Análisis y mapeo de esquemas
Fase 2 (Sem 5-12): Conversión de PL/SQL, migración de datos
Fase 3 (Sem 13-16): Pruebas de integración y rendimiento
Fase 4 (Sem 17-18): Migración en producción + soporte post-go-live

3. HERRAMIENTAS:
- Ora2Pg para conversión automática (~70% cobertura estimada)
- pgLoader para migración de datos
- AWS DMS para replicación durante cutover

4. RIESGOS:
- Incompatibilidad de tipos de datos Oracle-específicos (CLOB, RAW, INTERVAL)
- Diferencias en manejo de NULL/vacío en Oracle vs PostgreSQL
- Procedimientos con CONNECT BY (recursión) requieren reescritura
- Ventana de cutover limitada: máximo 4 horas (requerimiento del banco)

5. PRESUPUESTO: 380,000 EUR
6. GARANTÍA: 6 meses post go-live
---END DOCUMENT---

¿Es realista?
ASSISTANT: El timeline es ambicioso. 1,200 procedimientos PL/SQL en 8 semanas (Fase 2) con Ora2Pg al 70% significa ~360 procedimientos a convertir manualmente. Eso es 45 por semana. Para un banco, donde cada procedimiento puede tener lógica financiera crítica, es muy agresivo.
USER: ¿Y la ventana de cutover de 4 horas para 2.3 TB?
ASSISTANT: 2.3 TB en 4 horas requiere un throughput sostenido de ~590 GB/hora. Con AWS DMS y replicación previa, es factible si hacen CDC (Change Data Capture) durante semanas antes del cutover, dejando solo el delta final para la ventana. Pero necesitan ensayo general con datos reales.
USER: El problema es que Oracle tiene tablespaces encriptados. ¿DMS maneja eso?
ASSISTANT: AWS DMS puede leer de Oracle con TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) si configura el endpoint con las credenciales del wallet. Pero hay limitaciones: DMS no soporta ciertos tipos de LOB encriptados directamente. Necesitarán validar con un POC antes de comprometerse.
USER: Y los jobs de Oracle — 340 jobs a pg_cron. pg_cron es muy básico comparado con Oracle Scheduler.
ASSISTANT: Tiene razón. pg_cron no soporta cadenas de dependencias, calendarios complejos, ni monitoreo integrado. Para 340 jobs bancarios, necesitarán un scheduler externo como Apache Airflow o Control-M. pg_cron sólo sirve para jobs simples.
USER: Eso no está en la propuesta. ¿Qué más falta?
ASSISTANT: Faltan varios puntos críticos: 1) Plan de rollback — ¿qué pasa si falla el cutover?, 2) Validación de datos post-migración — comparación hash/checksum, 3) Plan de rendimiento — los query plans en PostgreSQL serán diferentes, necesitan tuning, 4) Cumplimiento regulatorio — BancoSur como banco necesita aprobación del regulador para cambiar infraestructura core.
USER: Excelente. Voy a pedir que amplíen la propuesta con estos puntos.
output:
TEMA: Revisión de propuesta de migración Oracle 12c → PostgreSQL 16 para BancoSur
DOCUMENTO_FUENTE: Propuesta de migración de DataMigrate Solutions para BancoSur S.A.
HECHOS_CLAVE_DEL_DOCUMENTO:
- Alcance: 847 tablas, 2.3 TB, 1,200 proc PL/SQL, 340 jobs, 89 materialized views
- Integraciones: 12 REST, 4 ETL batch, 2 SWIFT
- Timeline: 18 semanas (4 fases)
- Herramientas: Ora2Pg (70% cobertura), pgLoader, AWS DMS
- Cutover: máximo 4 horas (requisito del banco)
- Presupuesto: 380,000 EUR, garantía 6 meses
- Riesgos identificados: CLOB/RAW/INTERVAL, NULL handling, CONNECT BY
HALLAZGOS_DE_LA_CONVERSACIÓN:
- Fase 2 agresiva: ~360 procedimientos manuales = 45/semana (lógica financiera crítica)
- Cutover 4h para 2.3 TB factible solo con CDC previo + ensayo general
- Oracle TDE + AWS DMS: limitaciones con LOBs encriptados — requiere POC
- pg_cron insuficiente para 340 jobs bancarios — necesita Airflow o Control-M
- Gaps en propuesta: plan de rollback, validación datos post-migración, tuning PostgreSQL, aprobación regulatoria
FEEDBACK_USUARIO:
- "Oracle tiene tablespaces encriptados" (restricción técnica no contemplada)
- "pg_cron es muy básico" (confirmó que la propuesta subestima complejidad de jobs)
- "¿Qué más falta?" (pidió análisis de gaps completo)
ACCIONES:
- Solicitar ampliación de propuesta con: plan de rollback, validación post-migración, plan de rendimiento, cumplimiento regulatorio
- Exigir POC de DMS con TDE antes de comprometerse
- Reemplazar pg_cron por scheduler enterprise (Airflow/Control-M)
- Añadir ensayo general de cutover con datos reales
DECISIONES: Pedir propuesta ampliada antes de aprobar

USER: Я получил техзадание от заказчика на разработку мобильного приложения. Оцени его:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
ТЕХНИЧЕСКОЕ ЗАДАНИЕ
Заказчик: ООО «ФудЭкспресс»
Проект: Мобильное приложение доставки еды
Версия: 1.0 от 15.03.2026

1. ФУНКЦИОНАЛЬНЫЕ ТРЕБОВАНИЯ:
1.1 Каталог ресторанов с фильтрацией (кухня, рейтинг, время доставки, цена)
1.2 Корзина и оформление заказа
1.3 Онлайн-оплата: банковские карты, СБП, Apple Pay, Google Pay
1.4 Отслеживание курьера в реальном времени на карте
1.5 Push-уведомления о статусе заказа
1.6 Система лояльности: баллы, промокоды, реферальная программа
1.7 Чат с поддержкой и курьером
1.8 Оценка и отзывы после заказа
1.9 Личный кабинет: история заказов, адреса, способы оплаты

2. НЕФУНКЦИОНАЛЬНЫЕ:
2.1 Платформы: iOS 15+, Android 12+
2.2 Фреймворк: на усмотрение исполнителя
2.3 Время отклика API: <200ms (p95)
2.4 Нагрузка: до 10 000 одновременных пользователей
2.5 Доступность: 99.9%

3. СРОКИ: 4 месяца до MVP
4. БЮДЖЕТ: 8 000 000 руб.
---END DOCUMENT---

Что скажешь?
ASSISTANT: ТЗ покрывает основной функционал доставки еды, но есть проблемы. Главная: 4 месяца на MVP с полным функционалом (лояльность, чат, реферальная программа, реал-тайм отслеживание) — это не MVP, это полноценный продукт. Настоящий MVP — это каталог + корзина + оплата + уведомления.
USER: У нас команда из 6 разработчиков. Можем уложиться?
ASSISTANT: 6 разработчиков на 4 месяца — это 24 человеко-месяца. Для кроссплатформенного приложения (React Native/Flutter) с бэкендом это реально для MVP без пунктов 1.4, 1.6, 1.7. С полным ТЗ — нет.
USER: Заказчик настаивает на отслеживании курьера (1.4). Говорит это ключевая фича.
ASSISTANT: Отслеживание в реал-тайме — это отдельный сервис: WebSocket/SSE, интеграция с геолокацией курьера, отображение на карте. Минимум 3-4 недели для одного разработчика. Можно включить в MVP, но тогда убрать лояльность (1.6) и чат (1.7) из первой версии.
USER: А бюджет 8 млн — это достаточно?
ASSISTANT: Для 6 разработчиков на 4 месяца в Москве — это впритык (средняя зарплата мидл-разработчика ~250-350k/мес). 6 × 300k × 4 = 7.2М только зарплаты. Остаётся 800k на инфраструктуру, лицензии, тестирование. Очень мало.
USER: Мы работаем из Казани, ставки ниже.
ASSISTANT: В Казани средние ставки ~180-250k. 6 × 200k × 4 = 4.8М. Остаётся 3.2М на всё остальное — это уже комфортнее. Бюджет реалистичен для MVP из Казани.
USER: Хорошо. Тогда MVP без лояльности и чата, но с отслеживанием.
output:

ДОКУМЕНТ_ИСТОЧНИК: Техническое задание ООО «ФудЭкспресс» v1.0 от 15.03.2026

- Платформы: iOS 15+, Android 12+, фреймворк на усмотрение исполнителя
- NFR: API <200ms p95, 10k одновременных пользователей, 99.9% доступность
- Сроки: 4 месяца до MVP
- Бюджет: 8 000 000 руб.
ВЫВОДЫ_ИЗ_ОБСУЖДЕНИЯ:
- ТЗ описывает полноценный продукт, а не MVP
- Команда 6 человек из Казани — бюджет реалистичен (~4.8М зарплаты + 3.2М остальное)
- Отслеживание курьера (1.4) — ключевая фича по требованию заказчика, 3-4 недели работы
- Лояльность (1.6) и чат (1.7) убраны из MVP
- 24 человеко-месяца достаточно для урезанного MVP
ОБРАТНАЯ_СВЯЗЬ_ПОЛЬЗОВАТЕЛЯ:
- "Заказчик настаивает на отслеживании курьера" (коррекция приоритетов)
- "Мы работаем из Казани, ставки ниже" (коррекция расчёта бюджета)
ПЛАН_ДЕЙСТВИЙ:

РЕШЕНИЯ: MVP без лояльности (1.6) и чата (1.7), с отслеживанием курьера (1.4). Бюджет 8М реалистичен из Казани

USER: Хороший пункт. А что со сроками?
ASSISTANT: 16 недель на разработку: 47 edge-модулей + ML-модели + дашборд + SAP PM интеграция + мобильное приложение — это очень сжато. SAP PM интеграция в промышленной среде сама по себе может занять 4-6 недель. Какой размер вашей команды?
USER: 8 человек: 3 бэкенд, 2 ML-инженера, 1 фронтенд, 1 мобильный, 1 DevOps.

USER: Неустойка 0.1% в день — это нормально?
ASSISTANT: 0.1% в день от 42М — это 42 000 руб./день, или ~1.26М в месяц. Потолок 10% = 4.2М. Для IT-подряда 0.1% — это стандарт, потолок 10% тоже разумный. Это защищает вас от катастрофических штрафов.
USER: А оплата 30-40-30 — это нормальная схема?
ASSISTANT: Для IT-подряда 30-40-30 стандартно. Аванс 30% покрывает начальные расходы. Основная часть 40% привязана к акту приёмки этапа 2 — убедитесь, что критерии приёмки чётко прописаны, иначе заказчик может затягивать подписание акта.
USER: Верно. Критерии приёмки расплывчатые — "система функционирует в соответствии с ТЗ". Надо конкретизировать.
ASSISTANT: Обязательно. Пропишите: какие тесты, какие метрики, сколько дней на приёмку, что считается замечанием vs отказом, порядок устранения замечаний. Без этого вы рискуете не получить 40% месяцами.

ASSISTANT: Najpoważniejszy problem to punkt 1 — baza danych z danymi pacjentów jest publicznie dostępna z internetu bez VPN i bez szyfrowania at-rest. To naruszenie RODO w kontekście danych medycznych.

USER: Dostałem odpowiedź od prawnika w sprawie RODO, sprawdź:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
Kancelaria Prawna Nowak & Partnerzy
Opinia prawna nr KP/2026/089
Data: 2026-03-10

Dotyczy: Zgodność systemu CRM z RODO — przetwarzanie danych osobowych klientów

1. Podstawa prawna przetwarzania (art. 6 RODO)
Aktualnie system przetwarza dane na podstawie zgody (art. 6 ust. 1 lit. a). Rekomendujemy zmianę na prawnie uzasadniony interes (art. 6 ust. 1 lit. f) dla danych kontaktowych klientów B2B, co eliminuje wymóg zbierania zgód.

2. Retencja danych
Aktualny okres: bezterminowo. NIEZGODNE z RODO.
Rekomendacja: 3 lata od ostatniej aktywności klienta, następnie anonimizacja.

3. Prawo do usunięcia (art. 17)
System nie umożliwia pełnego usunięcia — dane pozostają w backupach.
Rekomendacja: Wdrożenie mechanizmu kaskadowego usuwania, w tym z backupów w ciągu 30 dni.

4. Rejestr czynności przetwarzania (art. 30)
Brak rejestru. WYMAGANY dla firm >250 pracowników.
Rekomendacja: Wdrożenie rejestru w ciągu 60 dni.

Pilność: WYSOKA — punkt 2 i 3 stanowią naruszenie RODO.
---END DOCUMENT---

Co myślisz?
ASSISTANT: Opinia wskazuje na dwa aktywne naruszenia RODO: bezterminowa retencja danych i brak mechanizmu pełnego usunięcia. To są najważniejsze do naprawienia.
USER: Ile mamy pracowników to nie ich sprawa, ale faktycznie mamy ponad 250. Ten rejestr jest aż tak ważny?
ASSISTANT: Tak, dla firm powyżej 250 pracowników rejestr czynności przetwarzania jest obowiązkowy wg art. 30 RODO. Brak rejestru to potencjalna kara.
USER: OK ale te 60 dni na rejestr to za długo, zróbmy to w 30. A co z tą zmianą podstawy prawnej na art. 6 f?
ASSISTANT: Zmiana na prawnie uzasadniony interes (art. 6.1.f) jest sensowna dla B2B — eliminuje zbieranie zgód. Ale wymaga przeprowadzenia testu równowagi interesów (balancing test) i udokumentowania go.
USER: Dobra, zaplanuj kolejność wdrożenia.
output:
TEMAT: Analiza opinii prawnej dot. zgodności CRM z RODO
DOKUMENT_ŹRÓDŁOWY: Opinia prawna KP/2026/089, Kancelaria Nowak & Partnerzy, 2026-03-10
KLUCZOWE_FAKTY_Z_DOKUMENTU:
- Podstawa prawna: obecnie zgoda (art. 6.1.a), rekomendacja: prawnie uzasadniony interes (art. 6.1.f) dla B2B
- Retencja: BEZTERMINOWA — naruszenie RODO; rekomendacja: 3 lata od ostatniej aktywności + anonimizacja
- Prawo do usunięcia (art. 17): brak kaskadowego usuwania z backupów; rekomendacja: 30 dni na usunięcie z backupów
- Rejestr czynności (art. 30): brak, wymagany dla >250 pracowników; rekomendacja: 60 dni
- Pilność: WYSOKA — punkty 2 i 3 to aktywne naruszenia
USTALENIA_Z_ROZMOWY:
- Firma ma ponad 250 pracowników — rejestr art. 30 obowiązkowy
- User chce rejestr w 30 dni (nie 60 jak rekomendacja)
- Zmiana na art. 6.1.f wymaga testu równowagi interesów
- Dwa aktywne naruszenia RODO wymagają natychmiastowej reakcji
FEEDBACK_UŻYTKOWNIKA:
- "60 dni na rejestr to za długo, zróbmy to w 30" — szybsze tempo niż rekomendacja
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Priorytet 1: wdrożyć politykę retencji (3 lata + anonimizacja)
- Priorytet 2: mechanizm kaskadowego usuwania z backupów
- Priorytet 3: rejestr czynności przetwarzania (30 dni)

DECYZJE: Skrócenie terminu rejestru z 60 do 30 dni

USER: Przeanalizuj tę specyfikację techniczną pod kątem naszego obecnego systemu:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
SPECYFIKACJA TECHNICZNA — System Kolejkowania Zleceń v2.0
Data: 2026-03-01
Autor: Dział Architektury

Wymagania:
1. Przepustowość: min. 10 000 msg/s (peak: 50 000 msg/s)
2. Latencja: p99 < 50ms
3. Gwarancja dostarczenia: at-least-once z deduplikacją
4. Retencja wiadomości: 7 dni
5. Format: Protobuf (schema registry)
6. Partycjonowanie: po tenant_id (min. 64 partycje)
7. Dead letter queue z auto-retry (3 próby, exponential backoff)
8. Szyfrowanie: TLS 1.3 in-transit, AES-256 at-rest
9. Monitoring: metryki per-partition lag, consumer group health
10. Kompatybilność: musi wspierać .NET 10 i Rust klientów

Proponowana technologia: Apache Kafka 3.8
---END DOCUMENT---

My teraz używamy RabbitMQ 3.12. Damy radę czy trzeba migrować?
ASSISTANT: RabbitMQ 3.12 może mieć problem z kilkoma wymaganiami: 50k msg/s na peaku to dużo dla Rabbit, Protobuf + schema registry to bardziej ekosystem Kafki, a 64 partycje z partycjonowaniem po tenant_id to natywna cecha Kafki.
USER: RabbitMQ z Quorum Queues daje 50k msg/s bez problemu, nie zgadzam się.
ASSISTANT: Ma Pan rację — Quorum Queues w RabbitMQ 3.12 mogą osiągnąć taką przepustowość przy odpowiedniej konfiguracji. Przepraszam za błąd. Główne problemy to raczej natywne partycjonowanie i schema registry.
USER: OK, to co proponujesz? Migracja na Kafkę czy rozbudowa Rabbita?
ASSISTANT: Biorąc pod uwagę wymagania 5 (Protobuf + schema registry) i 6 (64 partycji po tenant_id), Kafka 3.8 jest lepszym dopasowaniem. Migracja będzie kosztowna, ale RabbitMQ wymagałby wielu custom rozwiązań.
USER: A punkt 10 — mamy klienty .NET i Rust. Kafka ma dobre SDK dla obu?
output:
TEMAT: Analiza specyfikacji systemu kolejkowania v2.0 — Kafka vs RabbitMQ
DOKUMENT_ŹRÓDŁOWY: Specyfikacja techniczna "System Kolejkowania Zleceń v2.0", Dział Architektury, 2026-03-01
KLUCZOWE_FAKTY_Z_DOKUMENTU:
- Przepustowość: 10k msg/s (peak 50k)
- Latencja: p99 < 50ms
- Gwarancja: at-least-once + deduplikacja
- Format: Protobuf ze schema registry
- Partycjonowanie: tenant_id, min. 64 partycje
- DLQ z auto-retry (3×, exponential backoff)
- Szyfrowanie: TLS 1.3 + AES-256 at-rest
- Kompatybilność: .NET 10 i Rust
- Proponowana technologia: Kafka 3.8
USTALENIA_Z_ROZMOWY:
- Aktualny system: RabbitMQ 3.12
- RabbitMQ z Quorum Queues MOŻE osiągnąć 50k msg/s (korekta użytkownika)
- Główne bariery RabbitMQ: brak natywnego partycjonowania po tenant_id, brak schema registry
- Rekomendacja: migracja na Kafkę 3.8
- Otwarte pytanie: jakość SDK Kafki dla .NET 10 i Rust
FEEDBACK_UŻYTKOWNIKA:
- "RabbitMQ z Quorum Queues daje 50k msg/s bez problemu" — korekta błędnej oceny AI dot. przepustowości
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Sprawdzić dojrzałość klientów Kafka dla .NET 10 i Rust
- Zaplanować migrację RabbitMQ → Kafka 3.8
DECYZJE: Migracja na Kafkę 3.8 (wstępna decyzja, zależna od weryfikacji SDK)

USER: Check this vendor SLA and tell me if it's acceptable for our production environment:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
SERVICE LEVEL AGREEMENT
Provider: CloudStack Solutions Inc.
Client: [Your Company]
Service: Managed Kubernetes Platform
Effective: 2026-04-01

Uptime Commitment:
- Production cluster: 99.9% monthly uptime (max 43.8 min downtime/month)
- Staging/Dev: 99.5% (max 3.6 hours downtime/month)

Incident Response:
- P1 (service down): 15 min acknowledgment, 1 hour resolution target
- P2 (degraded): 30 min acknowledgment, 4 hour resolution target
- P3 (minor): 2 hour acknowledgment, next business day resolution

Credits:
- <99.9%: 10% monthly credit
- <99.5%: 25% monthly credit
- <99.0%: 50% monthly credit

Exclusions:
- Scheduled maintenance (up to 4 hours/month, 48h notice)
- Force majeure
- Client-caused incidents
- Third-party dependency failures
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: The SLA is mostly standard. Key concerns: the 99.9% uptime gives you ~44 minutes of downtime per month which may not be enough for critical production. The exclusion for "third-party dependency failures" is very broad — it could exclude cloud provider outages.
USER: We need 99.95% minimum. And that third-party exclusion is a dealbreaker.
ASSISTANT: Agreed. I'd negotiate: 1) Uptime to 99.95% (max 22 min/month), 2) Remove or narrow the third-party exclusion — at minimum, their core cloud provider should be covered, 3) Consider higher credits — 10% for missing 99.9% is low.
USER: Actually the credit structure is fine, I don't want to push on too many things. Focus on uptime and the exclusion.
ASSISTANT: Makes sense — pick your battles. Counter-proposal: 99.95% uptime for production, and replace "third-party dependency failures" with "third-party failures outside of provider's primary cloud infrastructure".
USER: Perfect. Also, that scheduled maintenance window — 4 hours per month seems like a lot. Can we get it down to 2?
ASSISTANT: 2 hours is reasonable for managed K8s. Most rolling updates take under an hour. I'd add that maintenance must happen during off-peak hours (e.g., 2-6 AM client timezone).
output:
TOPIC: Review of CloudStack Solutions SLA for Managed Kubernetes Platform
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Service Level Agreement, CloudStack Solutions Inc., effective 2026-04-01
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Production uptime: 99.9% (max 43.8 min downtime/month)
- Staging/Dev uptime: 99.5%
- Incident response: P1 15 min ack / 1h resolution, P2 30 min / 4h, P3 2h / next business day
- Credits: 10% (<99.9%), 25% (<99.5%), 50% (<99.0%)
- Scheduled maintenance: up to 4 hours/month, 48h notice
- Exclusions: maintenance, force majeure, client-caused, third-party dependency failures
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- 99.9% insufficient — need 99.95% minimum (max 22 min/month)
- "Third-party dependency failures" exclusion too broad — dealbreaker
- Credit structure acceptable — not worth negotiating
- 4 hours/month maintenance too much — target 2 hours during off-peak
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "I don't want to push on too many things" — strategic: focus on uptime + exclusion only
- "Perfect" — approved narrowed exclusion language
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Counter-propose: 99.95% production uptime
- Replace third-party exclusion with "failures outside provider's primary cloud infrastructure"
- Reduce maintenance window to 2 hours/month during off-peak (2-6 AM client timezone)
DECISIONS: Credit structure accepted as-is; three negotiation points identified

input:
USER: I got this error report from QA, can you analyze it?

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
BUG REPORT #4721
Reporter: Sarah Chen (QA Lead)
Date: 2026-03-25
Severity: Critical
Environment: Staging (v2.14.0-rc3)

Title: Payment processing fails silently for amounts > $9,999.99

Steps to reproduce:
1. Create order with total > $9,999.99
2. Proceed to checkout
3. Submit payment via Stripe
4. Order shows "Processing" but never completes

Expected: Payment processes successfully
Actual: Payment hangs in "Processing" state indefinitely

Notes:
- Works fine for amounts <= $9,999.99
- No errors in frontend console
- Stripe dashboard shows no attempt for these transactions
- Backend logs show: "PaymentService.ProcessAsync: amount validation passed" then nothing
- DB shows order status = 'payment_pending' with no further updates
- Affects ~12% of staging test orders (47 out of 390)
- First noticed after v2.14.0-rc2 → rc3 upgrade
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: This looks like a data type or formatting issue introduced in rc3. The $9,999.99 boundary strongly suggests a decimal parsing problem — likely the amount field is being formatted with a comma as thousands separator before being sent to Stripe, which would cause Stripe to reject or ignore it.
USER: Good hypothesis. But it says Stripe shows no attempt — so it's failing before reaching Stripe.
ASSISTANT: You're right — if Stripe sees nothing, the failure is between the backend validation (which passes) and the Stripe API call. Could be: 1) Amount serialization issue in the HTTP client, 2) A pre-flight validation that silently drops amounts with commas, or 3) An async task that fails to queue.
USER: Check what changed between rc2 and rc3. The diff should show something in PaymentService.
ASSISTANT: I don't have access to your codebase, but based on the pattern: look for changes in PaymentService.ProcessAsync between rc2 and rc3, specifically around amount formatting, serialization to Stripe's API (which expects amounts in cents as integers), or any new validation middleware.
USER: We switched from decimal to string for the amount field in rc3 for "flexibility". I bet that's it.
ASSISTANT: That's almost certainly the root cause. When amount is a string, values over 9,999.99 likely include formatting characters (comma, currency symbol) that either fail parsing silently or produce an invalid value that gets dropped before the Stripe call.
output:
TOPIC: Analysis of Bug #4721 — Silent payment failure for amounts > $9,999.99
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Bug Report #4721, reported by Sarah Chen (QA Lead), 2026-03-25
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Severity: Critical, Environment: Staging v2.14.0-rc3
- Payments > $9,999.99 hang in "Processing" state indefinitely
- Stripe dashboard shows NO attempt for affected transactions
- Backend logs: validation passes, then silence
- DB: order stays in 'payment_pending'
- 12% of staging orders affected (47/390)
- Regression: first seen after rc2 → rc3 upgrade
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Failure occurs BETWEEN backend validation and Stripe API call (Stripe never receives request)
- Root cause identified: amount field changed from decimal to string in rc3
- String amounts > 9,999.99 include comma formatting → invalid value → silently dropped
- The $9,999.99 boundary = thousands separator threshold
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "it says Stripe shows no attempt" — correction: failure is pre-Stripe, not at Stripe
- "We switched from decimal to string for the amount field in rc3" — user identified the code change
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Revert amount field from string back to decimal in PaymentService, or add proper parsing/sanitization
- Verify fix with amounts: $10,000+, $99,999+, $1,000,000+
- Add regression test for payment amounts crossing thousands separator boundaries
DECISIONS: Root cause confirmed as decimal→string type change in rc3

USER: Review this API response from our partner and tell me if it matches our integration spec:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
INTEGRATION SPECIFICATION v3.2
Endpoint: POST /api/v2/orders/sync
Expected Response (200 OK):
{
  "status": "success" | "partial" | "failed",
  "orders_processed": integer,
  "orders_failed": integer,
  "errors": [
    {
      "order_id": string,
      "code": string (enum: INVALID_SKU, OUT_OF_STOCK, PRICE_MISMATCH, DUPLICATE),
      "message": string
    }
  ],
  "sync_id": UUID,
  "timestamp": ISO-8601
}
---END DOCUMENT---

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
ACTUAL RESPONSE (captured 2026-03-26 14:32 UTC):
{
  "status": "partial",
  "processedOrders": 145,
  "failedOrders": 3,
  "errors": [
    {
      "orderId": "ORD-98234",
      "error_code": "STOCK_EMPTY",
      "msg": "Item SKU-44210 not available"
    },
    {
      "orderId": "ORD-98251",
      "error_code": "PRICE_DIFF",
      "msg": "Price mismatch for SKU-11092: expected 29.99, got 34.99"
    },
    {
      "orderId": "ORD-98255",
      "error_code": "INVALID",
      "msg": "Unknown SKU: SKU-00000"
    }
  ],
  "syncId": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-26T14:32:15Z"
}
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: Several mismatches between spec and actual response:
1. Field naming: spec uses snake_case (orders_processed), response uses camelCase (processedOrders)
2. Error code enum mismatch: spec says OUT_OF_STOCK, response has STOCK_EMPTY; spec says PRICE_MISMATCH, response has PRICE_DIFF; spec says INVALID_SKU, response has INVALID
3. Error object fields: spec has "code" and "message", response has "error_code" and "msg"
4. Error object: spec has "order_id" (snake_case), response has "orderId" (camelCase)
USER: The camelCase vs snake_case thing — is the sync_id also wrong?
ASSISTANT: Yes — spec says "sync_id", response has "syncId". So all field names are consistently camelCase in the response vs snake_case in the spec. This suggests the partner implemented using a different naming convention entirely.
USER: That's actually fine — we can handle both. The error codes are the real problem. Send them a list of what needs to change.
ASSISTANT: Got it — the naming convention difference is acceptable. The critical issues for the partner:
1. Error codes must match spec enum: STOCK_EMPTY → OUT_OF_STOCK, PRICE_DIFF → PRICE_MISMATCH, INVALID → INVALID_SKU
2. Error object fields: "error_code" → "code", "msg" → "message", "orderId" → keep as-is if camelCase accepted
USER: Wait, if we accept camelCase, then orderId is fine. Only fix error codes and the error field names.
output:
TOPIC: API response validation — partner order sync endpoint vs integration spec v3.2
SOURCE_DOCUMENTS:
- Integration Specification v3.2 (POST /api/v2/orders/sync)
- Actual response captured 2026-03-26 14:32 UTC
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENTS:
- Spec: snake_case fields (orders_processed, sync_id, order_id), error codes: INVALID_SKU, OUT_OF_STOCK, PRICE_MISMATCH, DUPLICATE
- Response: camelCase fields (processedOrders, syncId, orderId), error codes: STOCK_EMPTY, PRICE_DIFF, INVALID
- Spec error fields: code, message | Response error fields: error_code, msg
- Response data: 145 processed, 3 failed, sync_id a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Naming convention (camelCase vs snake_case): ACCEPTABLE — can handle both
- Error codes: MUST be fixed to match spec enum
- Error object field names: "error_code" → "code", "msg" → "message"
- "orderId" in camelCase: acceptable (camelCase convention accepted)
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "That's actually fine — we can handle both" — camelCase naming accepted
- "Only fix error codes and the error field names" — narrowed scope of fix request
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Send partner list of required changes:
  1. Error codes: STOCK_EMPTY→OUT_OF_STOCK, PRICE_DIFF→PRICE_MISMATCH, INVALID→INVALID_SKU
  2. Error field names: error_code→code, msg→message
- orderId stays camelCase (accepted)
DECISIONS: Accept camelCase naming convention; require error code enum and field name compliance only

USER: Can you look at this contract amendment and flag any issues?

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
AMENDMENT NO. 3 TO MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT
Date: March 15, 2026
Between: Apex Digital Ltd ("Provider") and Meridian Corp ("Client")
Reference: MSA-2024-0891

1. Section 4.2 (Pricing) is amended as follows:
   Original: "Monthly fee: $45,000 USD"
   Amended: "Monthly fee: $52,000 USD, effective April 1, 2026"

2. Section 7.1 (Scope of Services) — added:
   "Provider shall additionally deliver bi-weekly analytics reports covering user engagement, conversion funnels, and revenue attribution."

3. Section 9.3 (Liability Cap) is amended as follows:
   Original: "Total liability shall not exceed 12 months of fees paid"
   Amended: "Total liability shall not exceed 6 months of fees paid"

4. Section 11.2 (Termination for Convenience):
   Original: "Either party may terminate with 90 days written notice"
   Amended: "Client may terminate with 90 days notice. Provider may terminate with 30 days notice."

5. All other terms of MSA-2024-0891 remain unchanged.

Signatures: [pending]
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: Three red flags:
1. **Liability cap halved** (Section 9.3): From 12 to 6 months. This is a significant reduction in your protection.
2. **Asymmetric termination** (Section 11.2): They can leave in 30 days but you need 90. This is heavily in Provider's favor.
3. **Price increase without proportional value**: $7,000/month more (+15.5%) but only adds bi-weekly analytics reports.

USER: The analytics reports are actually worth a lot to us. The price increase is fine.
ASSISTANT: Understood — the price/scope change is acceptable. That leaves the liability cap reduction and asymmetric termination as the main concerns.
USER: Can they really just reduce the liability cap like that? We agreed to 12 months originally.
ASSISTANT: They can propose it in an amendment, but you don't have to accept. Since signatures are pending, you can counter-propose keeping the original 12-month cap. The liability cap is arguably the most important clause here.
USER: OK push back on both the liability cap and the termination asymmetry. Keep everything else.
output:
TOPIC: Review of Amendment No. 3 to MSA-2024-0891 (Apex Digital ↔ Meridian Corp)
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Amendment No. 3 to Master Services Agreement MSA-2024-0891, dated March 15, 2026
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Section 4.2: Monthly fee increased $45,000 → $52,000 (+$7,000/15.5%), effective April 1, 2026
- Section 7.1: Added bi-weekly analytics reports (engagement, funnels, revenue attribution)
- Section 9.3: Liability cap reduced from 12 months → 6 months of fees paid
- Section 11.2: Termination changed from symmetric 90 days → Client 90 days / Provider 30 days
- Status: Signatures pending
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Price increase + analytics reports: ACCEPTABLE (user confirms reports are valuable)
- Liability cap halved: UNACCEPTABLE — push back, keep original 12 months
- Asymmetric termination: UNACCEPTABLE — Provider gets 30 days vs Client 90 days
- Amendment is unsigned — can still negotiate
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "The analytics reports are actually worth a lot to us. The price increase is fine" — approved Sections 4.2 and 7.1
- "push back on both the liability cap and the termination asymmetry" — rejected Sections 9.3 and 11.2
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Counter-propose: keep Section 9.3 at 12-month liability cap
- Counter-propose: keep Section 11.2 symmetric (90 days for both parties)
- Accept Sections 4.2 and 7.1 as amended
DECISIONS: Accept price increase and scope addition; reject liability cap reduction and asymmetric termination

USER: I need help understanding this incident postmortem. We had a major outage last week:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
POSTMORTEM: Production Database Outage
Incident ID: INC-2026-0347
Date: 2026-03-20, 02:14 UTC — 2026-03-20, 05:47 UTC
Duration: 3 hours 33 minutes
Severity: SEV-1
Impact: 100% of API traffic returned 503 errors. ~18,000 users affected.

Timeline:
02:14 — Automated alert: PostgreSQL primary connection pool exhausted
02:18 — On-call engineer (Jake M.) paged
02:25 — Jake confirms: all 200 connections in use, 1,400 queries queued
02:31 — Attempted restart of connection pooler (PgBouncer) — no effect
02:45 — Escalated to database team
03:02 — DB team identifies: runaway migration job from deploy at 01:58 holding 180 connections
03:15 — Migration job killed. Connections freed.
03:15-05:47 — Recovery: stale connections cleared, replica sync verified, gradual traffic restoration

Root Cause:
Deploy pipeline triggered migration job at 01:58 that acquired 180 connections and held them due to a missing transaction timeout. The migration was a new index on `orders` table (450M rows) that ran ALTER TABLE without CONCURRENTLY flag.

Contributing Factors:
1. No connection limit per job in PgBouncer config
2. Migration ran during peak batch processing window (01:00-03:00 UTC)
3. No alerting on single-job connection count
4. Deploy pipeline allows migrations without review
---END DOCUMENT---

What should we focus on first?
ASSISTANT: Focus on the contributing factors in order of blast radius: 1) Add per-job connection limits in PgBouncer — prevents any single job from taking down the pool, 2) Require migration review in the deploy pipeline — catches issues like missing CONCURRENTLY, 3) Move migrations outside batch processing windows.
USER: The batch window is non-negotiable, we can't move it.
ASSISTANT: Understood. Then the fix is to ensure migrations never overlap with it. Options: migrations only deploy in a separate maintenance window, or the pipeline blocks migration deploys during 01:00-03:00 UTC.
USER: Blocking during that window makes sense. But what about the connection limit — what's a reasonable number?
ASSISTANT: For a pool of 200 connections, I'd suggest limiting any single job to 10-20 connections max. That leaves 180+ for application traffic. The exact number depends on your migration complexity, but 10 is a safe default.
USER: 10 seems too low for large migrations. Let's do 25, and also add the transaction timeout that was missing.
output:
TOPIC: Postmortem analysis — production database outage INC-2026-0347
SOURCE_DOCUMENT: Postmortem INC-2026-0347, 2026-03-20 (02:14–05:47 UTC)
KEY_FACTS_FROM_DOCUMENT:
- Duration: 3h 33m, SEV-1, 100% API downtime, ~18,000 users affected
- Root cause: Migration job at 01:58 held 180/200 connections, no transaction timeout
- Migration: ALTER TABLE on orders (450M rows) without CONCURRENTLY flag
- Timeline: alert 02:14 → paged 02:18 → root cause found 03:02 → job killed 03:15 → full recovery 05:47
- Contributing factors: no per-job connection limit, migration during batch window, no single-job alerting, no migration review in pipeline
FINDINGS_FROM_DISCUSSION:
- Priority 1: per-job connection limit in PgBouncer (set to 25)
- Priority 2: migration review requirement in deploy pipeline
- Priority 3: block migration deploys during 01:00–03:00 UTC batch window
- Add transaction timeout to prevent connection holding
- Batch processing window (01:00–03:00 UTC) is non-negotiable
USER_FEEDBACK:
- "The batch window is non-negotiable" — cannot reschedule batch processing
- "10 seems too low for large migrations. Let's do 25" — adjusted connection limit
ACTION_ITEMS:
- Set PgBouncer per-job connection limit to 25
- Add mandatory transaction timeout for migration jobs
- Add pipeline gate: block migration deploys during 01:00–03:00 UTC
- Require migration review before deploy
- Add alerting on single-job connection count
DECISIONS: 25 connection limit per job; block migrations during batch window; add transaction timeout

ASSISTANT: Einige Punkte zum Beachten:
1. **Staffelmiete**: Die jährlichen Erhöhungen von ~5.5-6% sind relativ hoch. Der Münchner Mietspiegel erlaubt maximal die ortsübliche Vergleichsmiete.
2. **Schönheitsreparaturen bei Auszug**: Starre Klauseln sind nach BGH-Rechtsprechung unwirksam. Achten Sie darauf, ob es Fristen gibt.
3. **Untervermietung nicht gestattet**: Nach §553 BGB haben Sie grundsätzlich Anspruch auf Erlaubnis zur Untervermietung bei berechtigtem Interesse.

USER: Die Kaltmiete von 1.680 für 78m² in Schwabing — ist das angemessen?
ASSISTANT: Das sind 21,54 EUR/m². Für Schwabing (Leopoldstraße) ist das im oberen Bereich, aber nicht unüblich für Neuvermietungen in dieser Lage. Der Münchner Mietspiegel 2025 liegt bei ca. 18-23 EUR/m² für vergleichbare Lagen.
USER: OK, der Preis ist in Ordnung. Aber die Staffelmiete stört mich. Kann ich die wegverhandeln?
ASSISTANT: Sie können versuchen, die Staffelmiete zu streichen oder zumindest die Erhöhungen zu reduzieren (z.B. auf 3% pro Jahr). Alternativ könnten Sie eine Kappungsgrenze nach Mietspiegel vorschlagen.
USER: Gut, und was ist mit der Kaution? 5.040 EUR auf einmal ist viel.
ASSISTANT: Im Vertrag steht bereits: zahlbar in 3 Raten. Das ist Ihr gesetzliches Recht nach §551 BGB — 3 gleiche Raten à 1.680 EUR. Die erste Rate ist bei Mietbeginn fällig.

USER: Regarde cette proposition commerciale et dis-moi si on doit accepter:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
PROPOSITION COMMERCIALE
Fournisseur: TechnoServe SARL, Lyon
Client: DataVision SAS
Référence: PC-2026-0445
Date: 18 mars 2026

Objet: Migration infrastructure cloud AWS → Azure

Périmètre:
- Audit de l'infrastructure existante (2 semaines)
- Plan de migration détaillé
- Migration de 34 services (12 EC2 → Azure VMs, 8 Lambda → Azure Functions, 6 RDS → Azure SQL, 4 S3 → Blob Storage, 4 autres)
- Tests de validation post-migration
- Formation équipe (3 jours)
- Support post-migration: 3 mois

Délai: 16 semaines
Équipe: 1 chef de projet + 3 ingénieurs cloud

Budget:
- Audit + Planning: 18 000 EUR HT
- Migration: 95 000 EUR HT
- Formation: 12 000 EUR HT
- Support 3 mois: 24 000 EUR HT
Total: 149 000 EUR HT

Conditions: 30% à la signature, 40% à mi-parcours, 30% à la recette
Garantie: 6 mois de support correctif inclus après recette
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: Points d'attention :
1. **Budget** : 149k EUR pour 34 services sur 16 semaines, soit ~4 382 EUR par service. C'est dans la fourchette standard.
2. **Formation 3 jours** : Potentiellement court pour une migration AWS→Azure complète. 5 jours serait plus adapté.
3. **Support post-migration** : 3 mois payants (24k) + 6 mois de garantie corrective = 9 mois au total. C'est correct.
4. **Paiement** : 30/40/30 est standard, mais le 30% initial (44 700 EUR) est un risque si le prestataire ne livre pas.

USER: Le budget me semble élevé. On peut négocier ?
ASSISTANT: Oui, quelques leviers : 1) Réduire le support payant de 3 à 2 mois (-8 000 EUR), 2) Inclure la formation dans le forfait migration, 3) Négocier un forfait global autour de 125-130k.
USER: Non, le support est important, je ne veux pas le réduire. Mais la formation à 12k pour 3 jours c'est cher.
ASSISTANT: 12k pour 3 jours de formation, c'est 4 000 EUR/jour. C'est effectivement au-dessus du marché (2 500-3 000 EUR/jour habituellement). Vous pourriez négocier à 8k pour 3 jours, ou demander 5 jours pour le même prix.
USER: 5 jours pour 12k, bonne idée. Et le calendrier de paiement — je préfère 20/30/50 pour avoir plus de levier.
ASSISTANT: 20/30/50 vous donne plus de contrôle — 50% à la recette motive le prestataire. C'est une bonne stratégie.

USER: Revisa esta propuesta de contrato de desarrollo de software:

---BEGIN DOCUMENT---
CONTRATO DE DESARROLLO DE SOFTWARE
Desarrollador: CodeCraft Solutions S.L., Madrid
Cliente: RetailPro S.A.
Fecha: 10 de marzo de 2026
Referencia: CDS-2026-078

Alcance:
- Desarrollo de plataforma e-commerce B2B personalizada
- Catálogo de 50.000+ productos con búsqueda avanzada
- Sistema de pedidos con aprobación multinivel
- Integración con SAP ERP (módulos MM, SD, FI)
- Panel de administración
- App móvil (iOS + Android)

Tecnología: .NET 10, React, PostgreSQL, React Native

Plazo: 9 meses (3 fases de 3 meses)
Equipo: 1 PM + 2 backend + 2 frontend + 1 mobile + 1 QA

Presupuesto:
- Fase 1 (Core + catálogo): 85.000 EUR
- Fase 2 (Pedidos + SAP): 110.000 EUR
- Fase 3 (Admin + móvil): 95.000 EUR
Total: 290.000 EUR + IVA

Propiedad intelectual: El código fuente será propiedad del cliente tras el pago final.
Garantía: 12 meses de corrección de bugs sin coste adicional.
Penalización por retraso: 0.5% del valor de la fase por semana de retraso (máx. 10%).
---END DOCUMENT---

ASSISTANT: Observaciones principales:
1. **Presupuesto**: 290k EUR para una plataforma B2B con integración SAP es razonable, pero la integración SAP (Fase 2) suele ser la más compleja y 110k podría quedarse corto.
2. **Plazo**: 9 meses es ambicioso para este alcance, especialmente con app móvil.
3. **Propiedad intelectual**: Buena cláusula — el código es del cliente tras pago final.
4. **Penalización**: 0.5%/semana con tope del 10% es estándar.
5. **Riesgo**: No hay cláusula de aceptación por fase.

USER: ¿Qué quieres decir con "aceptación por fase"?
ASSISTANT: Cada fase debería tener criterios de aceptación definidos y un proceso de validación formal antes de pasar a la siguiente. Sin esto, podrían entregar una Fase 1 incompleta y empezar la Fase 2, acumulando deuda técnica.
USER: Buena observación. Y la integración SAP — ¿por qué dices que 110k es poco?
ASSISTANT: La integración con 3 módulos SAP (MM, SD, FI) requiere conectores específicos, mapeo de datos complejo, y pruebas extensivas. Normalmente una integración SAP de este alcance cuesta 80-150k por sí sola, y aquí incluye también el sistema de pedidos con aprobación multinivel.
USER: Tienes razón. Vamos a pedir que la Fase 2 se divida en dos subfases: pedidos (60k) y SAP (80k). Así el total sube a 320k pero con menos riesgo.
ASSISTANT: Buena estrategia. La Fase 2 dividida en 2a (Pedidos, 60k) y 2b (SAP, 80k) da más visibilidad y control. El aumento de 30k está justificado por la complejidad SAP.
output:

DOCUMENTO_FUENTE: Contrato CDS-2026-078, CodeCraft Solutions S.L., 10 de marzo de 2026
HECHOS_CLAVE_DEL_DOCUMENTO:
- Alcance: plataforma e-commerce B2B, 50k+ productos, pedidos multinivel, SAP (MM/SD/FI), admin, app móvil
- Tecnología: .NET 10, React, PostgreSQL, React Native
- Plazo: 9 meses en 3 fases de 3 meses
- Equipo: 7 personas (PM, 2 backend, 2 frontend, 1 mobile, 1 QA)
- Presupuesto original: 290.000 EUR (Fase 1: 85k, Fase 2: 110k, Fase 3: 95k)
- PI: código propiedad del cliente tras pago final
- Garantía: 12 meses corrección bugs
- Penalización: 0.5%/semana, máx. 10% del valor de fase
CONCLUSIONES_DE_DISCUSIÓN:
- Falta cláusula de aceptación por fase — riesgo de deuda técnica acumulada
- Integración SAP con 3 módulos infravalorada en 110k (mercado: 80-150k solo SAP)
- Fase 2 a dividir en subfases para control de riesgo
- Presupuesto revisado: 320.000 EUR
FEEDBACK_USUARIO:
- "Buena observación" — validó necesidad de criterios de aceptación por fase
- "Tienes razón" sobre infravaloración SAP
ACCIONES:
- Dividir Fase 2: 2a Pedidos (60k) + 2b SAP (80k)
- Añadir criterios de aceptación formales para cada fase/subfase
- Presupuesto total ajustado: 320.000 EUR
DECISIONES: Fase 2 dividida, presupuesto +30k, criterios de aceptación requeridos
